mls.title: Chapter 2 - Introduction to ML System Design
Data engineering Fundamentals
mls.paragraph:
- Data can be stored in different formats, and a database for two types of processing: transactional and analytical
-  Different modes fo data passing : Data storage engines for historical data. and real time transport for streaming data.
- User generated data needs to be processed fast, though  system-generated data ie logs can be processed much slower and can be debugged using Logstash, Datadog , Logz.io (uses ML modles to  to help in processing massive amount of logs).
- System Logs should be stored in low-access storage as logs will increase rapidly, and old logs which arent of use, can be deleted.

mls.title: More data
mls.paragraph:
- Internal databases of companies stores "First party data", ie the data which companies stores about their users, which is used for training ML models and betterment of application.
- Second party data are data collected by another company of their customer and shared with the first company.
- Third party data are collected by companies from the public who aren't their direct customers.
- In smartphones, we have IDFA (ios) and AAID in android, unique id of phone to aggregate all activities from phone. Data is hopefully anonymized.

- In 2021, Apple made their IDFA opt-in to curb the usage of advertiser ID. This made third party data available from iphone less, and company used different technique. 
- For example, China's advertising association, a state-supported trade assciation for ad industry, invested in Device Fingerprinting system called CAID, that alllowed  apps like Tiktok and Tencent to keep tracking iPhone users.

mls.title: Data formats
mls.paragraph:
The process of converting a data structure or object state into a format that can be stored and trasmitted and reconstructed latter is called *data serialization*

There are many formats, like JSON (key value), CSV(row major), Parquet(binary, column major), Pickle(binary)

JSON is easy to read, but since there are text based, it is large in size. Also its difficult to change the schema

CSV are row based, which means consecutive items are placed in next row, while in Parquet which is column based, elements are in columns, next to each other.

When accessing data, row-based storage is best, as modern computers process sequential data more efficiently than non-sequential data, so a row-based table will be much faster to access than accessing column based table.

Lets take an example of a dataset of 1000 examples, with each one having 5 features. Each example will be in a row. so  a row-major format, CSV will be better to access **examples** like  i want the details for id 6.
Parquet will be better to access the features which are in coulmn like i want all the incomes.

### Example Dataset (10 Rows × 5 Columns)

| ID | Age | Income | Score | Purchased |
|----|-----|--------|-------|-----------|
| 1  | 22  | 25000  | 45    | No        |
| 2  | 25  | 32000  | 52    | Yes       |
| 3  | 47  | 58000  | 68    | Yes       |
| 4  | 52  | 60000  | 72    | No        |
| 5  | 46  | 52000  | 65    | Yes       |
| 6  | 56  | 61000  | 70    | No        |
| 7  | 28  | 35000  | 55    | Yes       |
| 8  | 33  | 42000  | 60    | No        |
| 9  | 36  | 48000  | 63    | Yes       |
| 10 | 40  | 50000  | 66    | No        |

Therefore having more features means its better to store in column-based formats. However with row-major formats, if you dont know the size of the row, you will have to read all the columns and then filter down to few columns which you need. 
Even if you know the size of that row, you still have to jump around memory, unable to take advantage of caching.

Row-major formats allows faster writes. An example, if we want to keep adding more user in above table, it will be much faster to write in to a file where data is already in row-major format.

So the key take away is, row-major formats are better when you have to do a lot of writes, whereas column-based ones are better when you have to a lot of column-based reads.

AWS recommends using Parquet format because "the parquet format is up to 2x faster to unload and consume up to 6x less storage in Amazon S3"

mls.title: Data Model
mls.paragraph:
Data model is how you choose to represent your data, it affects the problems your system can solve and the way your system is built.

Example would be a uber app, where you want to represent cars, people do not want to buy cars but to ride them, so its better to show the price, and what type are they.

There are 3 major models:
1) Relational model
2) NoSQL
3) Graph model

mls.title: Relational Model
mls.paragraph:
- Edgar F. Codd invented the relational model in 1970. Here, the data is organised into relations; each relation is a set of tuples. 
- A table is an accepted visual representation of a relation, and each row of the table makes up a tuple.
- Relations are unordered and can be shuffled both rows and columns, and it will remain the same relation.
- Data following the relational model is usually stored in file formats like CSV or Parquet.
- Data normalisation is used to normalize the relation to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity, though it depends on project architecture and priorities based on problem which is being solved.
- After normalisation, your data will be spread across different table. You can join the data from different relations back together , though joining can be expensive.
- To retrieve data, SQL is used, though the data model behind SQL has deviated from original relational model.
 For example, SQL tables can have row duplicates, whereas true relations cant contain duplicates.

mls.title: Some for SQL
mls.paragraph:
SQL is a **declarative language**, as opposed to python, which is an **imperative language**. 
- Imperative : you declare steps needed for action to be performed, and the system executes them to return an output
- Delcarative: you specify the output you want, and the system figures out the steps needed to get you the queried outputs.

There are lot of difficult queries that exist in companies, and ML is used to optimize those SQL quries too. As query optimization is the most challenging problems in database systems and normalization means that data is spread out on multiple relations, which makes joining them even harder. You only need one query optimizer and all your application can leverage it.

mls.title: Declarative ML
mls.paragraph:
Inspired by declarative, people looked into declarative ML. With a declarative ML system, users only need to declare the features schema and the task, and the system will figure out the best model and there is no need to write code to construct, train, and tune models when building a model.

Popular frameworks are Ludwig, H20 AutoML.

In H20 AutoML, we dont have to specify model structure or hyperparameters. It experiments with multiple model arch. and picks the best model given the features and the task

mls.title: Iris dataset example
mls.paragraph:
Here we are using iris dataset by Ronald Fisher. 
Each row = one flower 
Each column = a measurement

### Features:
sepal_length
sepal_width
petal_length
petal_width

### Target (what you're predicting): Species
which can be setos, versicolor, virginica

Example row:
```
sepal_length = 5.1
sepal_width  = 3.5
petal_length = 1.4
petal_width  = 0.2
species      = setosa
```

In AutoML we will pass the 80% training data to find the best model to predict and then test it on the 20% of test data which is the unseen data.
max_models parameter as the name says, the maximum number of model to try it on and seed is a random number, using which you can change the randomness of picking model.

it returns `aml` which have properties and functions like `leader`, which is basically the best model it chose and then you can do something like 

`aml.leader.explain(test)` to "Explain how the model is making predictions on unseen (test) data"
`aml.leader.model_performance(test)` to "Give model performance on unseen(test) data"

Try on google collab to get the results.
```
import h2o
from h2o.automl import H2OAutoML

h2o.init()

data = h2o.import_file("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/iris.csv")

train, test = data.split_frame(ratios=[0.8])

aml = H2OAutoML(max_models=10, seed=1)
aml.train(y="species", training_frame=train)

aml.leader.model_performance(test)
```

mls.title: Results
mls.paragraph:
The best model for predecting the `species` i got was `ModelMetricsMultinomialGLM: glm`
```
MSE: 0.032259848791633156
RMSE: 0.17961026917087217
LogLoss: 0.08841955203692148
Null degrees of freedom: 29
Residual degrees of freedom: 15
Null deviance: 66.36713375867689
Residual deviance: 5.3051731222152885
```
A clearer representation:
| Actual \ Predicted | setosa | versicolor | virginica | Total |
|-------------------|--------|------------|------------|-------|
| setosa            | 10     | 0          | 0          | 10    |
| versicolor        | 0      | 10         | 2          | 12    |
| virginica         | 0      | 0          | 8          | 8     |
|-------------------|--------|------------|------------|-------|
| Total             | 10     | 10         | 10         | 30    |


We can see how when predicting the versicolor species, it got 10 right but 2 got predicted as virginica, therefore 0.1**67** error.

mls.title: NoSQL
mls.paragraph:
It just started as No schema SQL but then everyone realised its just shifting the role of maintaining schema to other team. NoSQL,  have subtypes.
1) Document model
2) Graph model

Document model have key value and each document can have sub document. A document is continious string encoded as JSON, XML or a binary format like BSON (Binary JSON). Each document has a unique key.

Its less efficient than SQL to execute joins across documents compared to across tables because you have to retrieve all documents and then filter and probably sort or count too. 

To solve this problem, engines were built for example, grahQL which gives SQL kinda like retrieving process, kinda funny and its more efficient but then it also asks for schema, to give error and to query, so its back to square one with this.

Though on the bright side, its easier to change schema early and easier to make edits in the schema.

mls.title: Graph model
mls.paragraph:
A graph model is built around the concept of "graph". Where nodes are the data and edges define the relationship. The relationships between data items or nodes are the priority in the graph model.

Its faster to retrieve data based on relationships. Many queries that are easy to do in one data model are harder to do in another data model. Picking the right data model for your application can make your life so much easier.

mls.title: Storing and processing data
mls.paragraph:
Data can be either structured or unstructured. Structured data has a schema, using which it can be analyzed much faster; in simple words, it has  a pattern to recognize. Whereas unstructured data doesn't have a schema, though it might still consist of some intrinsic patterns that help you extract structured data from it.

- A repository for storing structured data is called **data warehouse**
- A repository for storing unstructured data is called **data lake**. 
- Data lake usually used to store raw data before processing.
- Data warehoused are used to store data which have been processed into formats which are ready to use.
- Database are also known as storage engines, which are optimized for two types of worloads, **transactional processing** and **analytical processing**

mls.title: Transactional data
mls.paragraph:
Transactional data is a type of data that refers to any kind of action: tweeting, watching youtube video or uploading.
The transactions are inserted as the yare generated and ocassionally updated when something changes or deleted when they are no longer needed. This type of processing is known as **online transaction processing (OLTP)**

Therefore **low latency** and **high availability**. **ACID is followed**

Atomicity: All steps in a transaction should be completed successfully; if any step fail, all other steps should fail too.
Consistency: There must always be predefined rules that the transaction will follow
Isolation: Guarantees two transactions happening at the same time as if they were isolated. For example, you don't want two users to book the same ride.
Durability: Once a transaction is completed and has been committed, it will remain committed even in the case of system failure.

Transactional DB doesn't need to be fully ACID, or to be ACID as some developers find it too restrictive.
According to Martin Kleppmann, systems that do not meet the ACID criteria are sometimes called BASE (Basically Available, soft state and eventual consistency).

Because each transaction is often processed as a unit separately from other transactions, transactional DB are often row-major.
Therefore, transactional DBs are not efficient for questions such as "What's the average price for all rides to Kolkata?" as this type of question, involves aggregating columns across different rows for a solution. As we know its easier to write row wise to data, but for an analytical question like this we might need column based, referring to [slide 7](https://plog-alpha.vercel.app/mls/7)

mls.title: Analytical Data
mls.paragraph:
Analytical data like the previous slide need analytical database that is efficient with queries that allow you to look at data from different viewpoints. This processing is called **OLAP (Online analytical processing)**

Due to limitations in technology, they have always been separated, and their usage has gone downhill. Nowadays, transactional DB can also handle analytical queries, such as CockroachDB, and vice versa, such as Apache Iceberg.

Secondly, traditionally OLAP or OLTP paradigms, storage and processing are tightly coupled-how data data is stored is also how data is processed. This result in same data being stored in multiple db and using different processing engines to solve different types of queries.

In the last decade, this changed, and storage has been decoupled from processing  (aka compute), adopted by many vendors including Google's Big Query, Snowflake, IBM. So the new way is to store data in the same place, with a processing layer on top that can be optimized for different types of queries.

*By the way, online in OLAP means user queries regarding multidimensional data are processed immediately, allowing for rapid, ad-hoc data exploration*

mls.title: ETL (Extract, Transform, Loading)
mls.paragraph:
- You decide on different sources of data to want from, mostly data is structured beause it comes from **data warehouse**
- validation is done, and reject the data that doesn't meet your criteria and notify the sources.
- Transformation begins, and data goes through a pipeline that consists of different operations such as transpose, deduplication, sorting, aggregation, custom validations, and deriving new features.
- Loading is deciding how and how often to load your transformed data into the target destination which can be another data warehouse or a database.
